title: Space dyeing with plant dyes
subtitle: Applying multiple plant dye colours to a single skein for a multi-colour result
excerpt: Space dyeing places different dye colours at intervals along a skein, producing a yarn that knits or weaves up in irregular multi-colour sections. This entry covers the bundling method, using at least three plant dye baths in sequence, and keeping the colour areas distinct rather than muddied.
sourceNotes: Space dyeing with plant dyes synthesised from traditional British dye practice. The bundling and resist method for keeping colour sections separate is described in general natural dye handbooks.

glossaryTerms.definition: A metallic salt that bonds dye to fibre. For space dyeing, the whole skein is mordanted before any dye is applied so all sections will take colour.
A pot of extracted dye solution. In space dyeing, a separate dye bath is prepared for each colour section of the skein.
A barrier that prevents dye penetrating a specific section of the yarn. In space dyeing, tightly wound rubber bands or sections folded in plastic keep colour sections separate during dyeing.

content.text: Space dyeing gives a single skein multiple distinct colour sections. With plant dyes, each section needs its own
dye-bath
. The key challenge is preventing adjacent colours from bleeding into each other during heating. A light
resist
between sections, and working quickly when transferring the skein between baths, keeps the colour areas clean.

attrs.title: Mordant safety
attrs.body: Use dedicated dye-pots per colour. Wear gloves and apron throughout. Keep all dye materials away from food preparation areas.

items.name: Plant dye baths, 2 or 3 prepared separately (e.g. onion skins, weld, madder)

content.text: Method
Step 1: Prepare the dye baths

Prepare two or three plant dye baths separately before beginning. Each bath should be strained and ready at the working temperature of 70 to 80°C when the skein enters. Keep each bath warm on a low heat. Suggested colour set: onion skins (gold), weld (clear yellow), and walnut hulls (warm brown) give a range that complements without muddying.
Step 2: Divide the skein

Divide the mordanted, damp skein into sections by tying loose string around the boundary points. For three colours, tie at the one-third and two-thirds points around the skein loop. The string markers help you see where to apply each colour; they do not act as a resist by themselves.
Step 3: Dye the first section
Place the first third of the skein into the first
dye-bath

and fold the remaining two-thirds of the skein into a flat parcel. Wrap the out-of-bath section loosely in a piece of plastic film or place it in a small plastic bag to keep it damp and protected from splashing dye. Hold the dyed section in the bath at 75°C for 30 minutes.
Step 4: Dye the remaining sections

Remove the first section from the bath, allowing it to drain briefly. Transfer the second section into the second dye bath and fold the first and third sections outside the pot. Repeat for the third section. Work quickly between baths to prevent the boundary areas from drying out. The boundary zones between colours will blend slightly; this is normal and gives a natural transition rather than a sharp line.
Wastewater disposal
Dispose of all plant dye baths and the alum
mordant
bath via the foul drain or compost in small home quantities. Do not pour multiple baths into a storm drain, a watercourse, or a soakaway.

items.problem: The colour sections have blurred together into a single muddy colour
items.cause: The skein was not protected between baths, and dye from each bath mixed in the damp fibre
items.fix: Wrap each out-of-bath section in plastic film before entering the adjacent section into its bath. The physical barrier prevents dye migration at the boundaries.
